completenessNote: Curated summary of the stage model and progression logic. The source is very long and repetitive by stage, so the summary compresses each stage to its main operating constraint.

items.title: Scaling is a stage progression, not one generic problem
problemsSolved: Helps operators solve the right bottleneck for their current size instead of borrowing advice from a different stage.
items.text: The roadmap organizes growth into explicit stages, each with its own role, headcount, constraint, and graduation standard. Advice that works one stage later can be wasteful or harmful now.
usageExample: A solo founder with no sales should not prioritize executive scorecards before proving that anyone will pay for the offer.
sourceNotes: Sections: Start Here; Scaling Roadmap overview

items.title: Stage 0: Improvise
problemsSolved: Helps pre-revenue operators focus on getting people to try the thing instead of pretending they already run a company.
items.text: Stage 0 is about free usage, feedback, simple tools, and learning what is worth charging for. The goal is evidence of interest, not polished infrastructure.
usageExample: A new consultant can offer early free engagements, gather feedback, and learn what clients actually value before formal pricing and systems.

items.title: Stage 1: Monetize
problemsSolved: Helps early operators shift from free validation to first paid demand.
items.text: The main job is to make the first sale consistently, build a version-one product that is good enough to pay for, and learn to ask for money. Proof from free users becomes the first sales asset.
usageExample: A creator with a free community can launch a simple paid cohort and use feedback plus early wins to validate willingness to pay.

items.title: Stage 2: Advertise
problemsSolved: Helps businesses move from founder hustle to repeatable demand creation.
items.text: Once people will pay, the next step is learning how to generate demand systematically. The stage exists to make customer flow less dependent on sporadic referrals or personal outreach alone.
usageExample: A services business that can already sell from warm leads can begin testing ads or content systems that produce opportunities on demand.

items.title: Stage 3: Stabilize
problemsSolved: Helps businesses stop breaking delivery and operations every time demand increases.
items.text: At this point the company must make the existing motion more reliable: delivery quality, customer experience, basic systems, and economic consistency matter more than raw experimentation.
usageExample: A fast-growing agency can pause before the next growth push to standardize onboarding, reporting, and fulfillment quality.

items.title: Stage 4: Prioritize
problemsSolved: Helps leaders avoid being busy across too many initiatives when the company finally has enough activity to create real trade-offs.
items.text: The roadmap implies that scaling starts to fail when everything looks important at once. Prioritization becomes the move that protects the highest-leverage work from organizational noise.
usageExample: A founder with several channels, offers, and hires in flight can cut half the initiatives and concentrate on the one constraint most likely to unlock the next stage.

items.title: Stage 5: Productize
problemsSolved: Helps owner-dependent delivery become a standardized, repeatable product.
items.text: As businesses scale, custom work and founder improvisation become expensive. Productizing creates consistency, easier training, and cleaner economics.
usageExample: A consulting business can turn one-off solutions into clearer packages, playbooks, and standardized delivery paths.

items.title: Stage 6: Optimize
problemsSolved: Helps the company improve the efficiency of proven systems before chasing new complexity.
items.text: Optimization is about tightening the machine: conversion rates, margins, staffing, retention, and cycle times. Small percentage improvements matter more once the base is larger.
usageExample: An ecommerce company can improve checkout conversion, return rates, and repeat purchase before launching another channel.

items.title: Stage 7 and 8: Categorize then Specialize
problemsSolved: Helps larger companies organize talent and functions clearly enough to support specialization without chaos.
items.text: These later stages imply a shift from generalist hustle to role clarity, clearer ownership, and more specialized teams. The org has to know who owns what before deeper specialization adds value.
usageExample: A growing portfolio brand can split acquisition, retention, ops, and finance ownership cleanly before hiring more narrowly specialized leaders inside those functions.

items.title: Stage 9: Capitalize
problemsSolved: Helps mature businesses convert operational strength into enterprise value and compounding opportunity.
items.text: The final stage is about extracting the full strategic and financial upside of a well-built business. The company is no longer just surviving stage by stage; it is positioned to compound, invest, and capitalize on scale.
usageExample: A mature company with stable systems, management depth, and strong unit economics can pursue rollups, new capital structures, or more aggressive expansion from a position of strength.
